frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: Normative posterior computation that human judgments are compared to.

frameworks.name: amortized inference / recognition model
frameworks.one_line: Query-to-posterior mapping whose parameters are optimized for expected accuracy.

frameworks.name: resource-rational analysis
frameworks.one_line: Limited compute allocated preferentially to high-probability queries.

core_question: Why do humans systematically deviate from Bayesian inference, and when do they under-react to data versus prior?
key_assumptions: Brain implements a recognition model mapping queries to posteriors
Recognition-model parameters optimize average closeness to the true posterior
Computational resources are limited and allocated by query probability
manipulations: Query distribution shaped to favor different question types
key_findings: People under-react to data or to prior in patterned ways
These two underreactions can be controlled by changing the query distribution
interpretations: Inference is amortized: the brain learns to infer by adapting to the distribution of queries it faces
Memory effects, belief bias, and response variability fall out of the same recognition-model account
